(born 13 February 1984) is a British former [professional boxer](/wiki/Professional_boxer "Professional boxer") who competed from 2006 to 2016. He held the [British](/wiki/British_Boxing_Board_of_Control "British Boxing Board of Control") [super-featherweight](/wiki/Super-featherweight "Super-featherweight") title twice between 2010 and 2014, and challenged for the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_Boxing_Council "Commonwealth Boxing Council") super-featherweight and [lightweight](/wiki/Lightweight "Lightweight") titles in 2014 and 2016, respectively.[[1]](#cite_note-BoxRec:_Gary_Sykes-1)

**Gary Sykes** (born 13 February 1984) is a British former [professional boxer](/wiki/Professional_boxer "Professional boxer") who competed from 2006 to 2016. He held the [British](/wiki/British_Boxing_Board_of_Control "British Boxing Board of Control") [super-featherweight](/wiki/Super-featherweight "Super-featherweight") title twice between 2010 and 2014, and challenged for the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_Boxing_Council "Commonwealth Boxing Council") super-featherweight and [lightweight](/wiki/Lightweight "Lightweight") titles in 2014 and 2016, respectively.[[1]](#cite_note-BoxRec:_Gary_Sykes-1)

## Amateur career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Sykes&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Amateur career")]

Sykes boxing for Cleckheaton ABC[[2]](#cite_note-r1-2) enjoyed a 62 fight career as an amateur, a record which included at one stage a 32 fight winning streak as well as a victory over the current European champion at lightweight, [John Murray](/wiki/John_Murray_(boxer) "John Murray (boxer)"). During his time as an amateur Sykes also won titles in the junior ABA's as well as the full [ABA title](/wiki/ABA_Championships "ABA Championships") in 2005 beating Mike Robinson in the 60 kg category in the event held at the Excel Arena in London.[[2]](#cite_note-r1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-r2-3) Despite establishing himself as one of England's top amateurs, Sykes missed out on a chance to go to the 2006 Commonwealth Games in Melbourne after [Stephen Smith](/wiki/Stephen_Smith_(boxer) "Stephen Smith (boxer)"), another star amateur decided to move up and compete in the same weight division as Sykes. Speaking of the uncertainty at the time Sykes trainer [Keith Tate](/wiki/Keith_Tate "Keith Tate") said "everything is uncertain...because Smith's moved up to featherweight, and I know he's highly rated by Terry Edwards...I think Gary's earned his selection already but I have a feeling that this is going to come down to the next ABA Championships later this year....It seems like they're [moving the goalposts](/wiki/Moving_the_goalposts "Moving the goalposts") and I'll feel sorry for Gary if he doesn't get the opportunity. He's had a great year."[[4]](#cite_note-4) At the 2006 ABA championships, actually held on 2 December 2005 so as to allow preparation for the Commonwealth Games. Smith beat [Akaash Bhatia](/wiki/Akaash_Bhatia "Akaash Bhatia") in the final of the 57 kg.[[2]](#cite_note-r1-2)

## Professional career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Sykes&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Professional career")]

Sykes made his professional boxing debut on 23 February 2006 at the Town Hall in [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds") fighting journeyman Dave Hinds and running out a winner over six rounds. Sykes followed up the victory with on further win in 2006, defeating Dai Davies once again at the Town Hall with a third-round knockout. In 2007 Sykes defeated Kristian Laight, Eduards Krauklis, Deniss Sirjatovs, Dwayne Hill and Carl Allen fighting in venues across the north of England including [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley "Barnsley"), [Wigan](/wiki/Wigan "Wigan"), [Doncaster](/wiki/Doncaster "Doncaster") and [Altrincham](/wiki/Altrincham "Altrincham").[[5]](#cite_note-boxrec.com/list_bouts.php?human_id=349639&cat=boxer-5) In 2008 Sykes continued his winning streak with wins over Peter Allen, Harry Ramogoadi and Jason Nesbitt before starting 2009 on a high with a win over journeyman Johnny Greaves.[[5]](#cite_note-boxrec.com/list_bouts.php?human_id=349639&cat=boxer-5)

### British Champion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Sykes&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: British Champion")]

On 29 May 2009 Sykes faced his biggest challenge so far against [Anthony Crolla](/wiki/Anthony_Crolla "Anthony Crolla") in an eliminator for British super featherweight Title, winning over the 10 round distance. Two more victories followed against Ibrar Riyaz and Dean Mills before getting the chance to fight for the title on 5 March 2010. The fight, against [Andy Morris](/wiki/Andy_Morris_(boxer) "Andy Morris (boxer)") a former British featherweight champion, resulted in a close points win over 12 rounds at the Huddersfield Leisure Centre.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Morris, who also won a Commonwealth games bronze medal as an amateur, had stepped in at last notice following the withdrawal of original opponent [Ricky Burns](/wiki/Ricky_Burns "Ricky Burns").[[7]](#cite_note-7) On 28 May 2010 Sykes made a successful first defence of his belt beating Kevin O'Hara again over 12 rounds at the Huddersfield Leisure Centre.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Prizefighter tournament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Sykes&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Prizefighter tournament")]

On 20 November 2010 Sykes chose to enter the knock out style [Prizefighter series](/wiki/Prizefighter_series "Prizefighter series") promoted by [Barry Hearn](/wiki/Barry_Hearn "Barry Hearn") and televised live by [Sky Sports](/wiki/Sky_Sports "Sky Sports"). The competition, which would provide the winner a prize of £32,000, pitched Sykes against former British and European title challenger [Scott Lawton](/wiki/Scott_Lawton_(boxer) "Scott Lawton (boxer)") in the quarter finals in a bout which he comfortably won. In the semi-final Sykes, who had wanted to enter the tournament in order to raise his profile, faced Welshman [Gary Buckland](/wiki/Gary_Buckland "Gary Buckland") and suffered a surprise knockout defeat after just 45 seconds of the first round, the first defeat of his career. Luckily for Sykes, because of the format of the competition, his British title was not on the line. Buckland, himself a former European title challenger, went on to win the tournament beating [Derry Mathews](/wiki/Derry_Mathews "Derry Mathews") in the final.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Title defences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Sykes&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Title defences")]

On 5 March 2011 Sykes returned to the ring for the second defence of his British title against former champion [Carl Johanneson](/wiki/Carl_Johanneson "Carl Johanneson") of Leeds, the [mandatory challenger](/wiki/Mandatory_challenger "Mandatory challenger") for the title.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The fight, at the Huddersfield Sports Centre, resulted in a close points victory for Sykes after suffering a knockdown in the ninth round, eventually running out a 116–112, 115–112 and 115–113 winner. After the fight Sykes paid tribute to former champion Johanneson saying "To beat him means the world to me...when I first sparred with him I never thought I could last 12 rounds let alone beat him."[[11]](#cite_note-11) For his third defence Sykes faced [Gary Buckland](/wiki/Gary_Buckland "Gary Buckland") with the two meeting on 24 September 2011. In a close back and forth contest Sykes lost the title to Buckland with the Welshman being awarded the decision over 12 rounds resulting in a second career defeat for Sykes.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Retirement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Sykes&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Retirement")]

On 18 September 2016, Sykes announced his retirement from boxing on his Facebook page. He cited a lack of hunger and desire at his older age, and a lack of motivation to continue to fight.
